For production TLS changes that can break client compatibility, the absence of documented staged testing or rollback guidance in the ticket and relationships represents an operational risk — future changes should include mandatory compatibility validation (Report Designer and browser tests across supported clients), versioned configuration snapshots, and an approved rollback procedure recorded in the change ticket before applying IISCRYPTO or similar tooling [Data: Entities (0,480,18); Relationships (9,34,35)]. ## Recommended immediate actions: formalize TLS change control, add AOS/IIS restart playbook, and update monitoring/alerts Given the documented remediation and dependencies, the highest-priority operational actions are: 1) formalize a TLS/cipher-suite change control process that enforces synchronized configuration across UAT and PROD and requires pre-change validation; 2) add a concise runbook for TLS changes that includes steps to use IISCRYPTO, how and when to restart IIS and AOS servers, and post-change verification for report access; and 3) tune monitoring/alerts to detect report designer failures, unknown report-owner symptoms, and TLS negotiation failures so teams can detect regressions quickly. Implementing these controls will reduce time-to-detect and time-to-recover for similar incidents and make cross-team communication and escalation more predictable. ## Governance and long-term mitigation: standardize TLS baselines, document supported client profiles, and maintain a cross-region configuration registry The incident demonstrates the need for organizational governance around TLS baselines (approved cipher suites and minimum TLS versions) and explicit documentation of supported client versions for the Report Designer and browser clients. Records show the problem manifested across regions and environments and was tied to cipher-suite changes, reinforcing that a single source of truth for TLS configuration and supported client compatibility is required [Data: Entities (480,483); Relationships (803,796,36)]. A practical control is a configuration registry (or CMDB extension) that records TLS/cipher settings per environment and a pre-deployment verification checklist that gates any change; combine this with scheduled compatibility testing against a matrix of client versions to avoid breaking production users when adopting "best-practice" cryptographic settings that may remove older ciphers still required by legacy clients [Data: Entities (480,483); Relationships (36,796)]. ## Communication and escalation runbook: define stakeholder matrix and cross-reference internal tracking systems The records link the ticket to named customer contacts (Contact Person 7, Regina Jagsch), a consulting company, and an internal JIRA that reported the same error, indicating multiple stakeholders and tracking systems were in use during the incident [Data: Entities (2,11,24,17); Relationships (1,10,16,33)]. To streamline future incidents, create a short escalation runbook that maps roles (customer contact, platform-owner, consulting SPOC, security owner), primary communication channels, and which external/internal ticketing system must be updated (portal ticket and JIRA). Include templates for status updates and a requirement to escalate to platform engineering when TLS/cipher changes are proposed, ensuring a single coordinated change window and clear ownership for rollback and postmortem actions [Data: Entities (0,2,11,24,17); Relationships (1,10,16,33)].

REPORT 53: # Financial Reporting Service — Report Designer and AOS (001-SRV-AOS72/73) The community centers on REPORT DESIGNER (the client/editor) and the FINANCIAL REPORTING SERVICE runtime, which depend on AOS application servers (notably 001-SRV-AOS72 and 001-SRV-AOS73), the Data Mart/FRDB, SQL, and network/TLS/authentication plumbing. Users experience globally slow startup and rendering, intermittent "Report cannot be found" errors, and lost author metadata (authors shown as "unknown"). Remediation activity included FRDB recreation and Data Mart resets in the D365FO_INTE environment and a Microsoft support ticket was opened to investigate the global failures. Observations point to a mix of node-specific application-server degradation (72 vs 73), dependency failures affecting metadata lookup (service account/TLS/permissions), and potential network/latency factors that together produce the end-user symptoms. ## REPORT DESIGNER is tightly coupled to multiple backend dependencies (AOS, Data Mart/FRDB, SQL, and TLS/auth) and exhibits systemic symptoms. REPORT DESIGNER’s correct operation depends on successful TLS negotiation, service-account authentication/authorization, and reliable connectivity to AOS, the Data Mart/FRDB, and SQL. The symptom set reported (global slowness, "Report cannot be found", and missing author metadata) aligns with failures in one or more of those dependencies rather than purely a local client issue. This dependency surface makes the client sensitive to infrastructure, identity/auth, and database issues at multiple layers. The source material explicitly cites TLS compatibility, service account authentication, and reachability to AOS/Data Mart/SQL as required dependencies and diagnostic vectors for the observed problems. This implies remediation must treat authentication, certificate/TLS compatibility, and backend reachability as first-class troubleshooting targets rather than focusing solely on client-side tuning or UI fixes. [Data: Entities (34,477); Relationships (791,785)] ## Node-specific issue: 001-SRV-AOS72 behaves as a diagnostic fast-path while 001-SRV-AOS73 is sluggish — indicating a per-node degradation or configuration drift. When REPORT DESIGNER is launched directly on 001-SRV-AOS72 the client is performant, while normal (remote) access that routes through the service tier encounters global slowness and degraded behavior. This divergence between twin siblings suggests either a node-specific configuration, caching, or connectivity difference on 001-SRV-AOS73 (or the network path to it) rather than an application-wide bug. Relationships and entity descriptions identify 001-SRV-AOS72 as the node that returns acceptable performance and 001-SRV-AOS73 as the sluggish sibling implicated in degraded end-user experience; the pair are explicitly described as sibling application servers in the same tier. Investigation should therefore include node configuration comparison, local caches, certificate stores, service-account context, and network path/middleware (load balancer or firewall) differences between the two servers. [Data: Entities (35,36); Relationships (61,55)] ## Author metadata loss points to metadata store access or permission issues affecting existing reports. Reports are showing authors as "unknown", which is consistent with metadata being inaccessible or lost from the source store (or being present but unreadable due to permission/identity failures). The presence of a "PREVIOUS REPORT CREATORS" group and the explicit relationship noting these creators are now displayed as unknown indicate that author metadata either wasn’t migrated, became orphaned, or cannot be read by the reporting runtime due to permission or authentication issues. Restoring or validating metadata read paths requires validating the service account(s) used by the Financial Reporting Service and Report Designer have read access to metadata stores, and confirming that any database/schema changes (including FRDB recreation) preserved or remapped author identity attributes. Audit the metadata access patterns and permission mappings to determine whether the metadata still exists but is blocked, or was removed during prior maintenance. [Data: Entities (45,34); Relationships (58)] ## FRDB recreation and Data Mart resets in D365FO_INTE were used as remediation; this suggests data-store corruption or misalignment was suspected/observed. The FRDB instance in the D365FO_INTE environment was recreated by an engineer as part of remediation steps and the environment was used to manage FRDB/Data Mart artifacts. Recreating FRDB indicates the remediation team suspected the database schema or contents were out-of-sync or corrupted for reporting consumption, and used the integration/test environment to rebuild report data artifacts. While FRDB recreation can resolve schema or corruption issues, it also risks metadata loss or identity remapping if not coordinated with metadata and author attribution preservation. Any further remediation should include metadata validation, backups, and a reconciliation step to ensure recreated FRDB instances contain the expected author and metadata references or that mappings are restored. [Data: Entities (38,37); Relationships (60,62)] ## Network latency is an acknowledged contributing factor and must be ruled in/out as part of root-cause analysis. Network latency is explicitly included in the decision tree as a cause for extreme startup slowness of Report Designer. Given the multi-tier traffic pattern (client → Reporting runtime → AOS → Data Mart/SQL) even modest latency or intermittent path degradation can amplify startup delays and timeouts, worsening perceived performance. Key infrastructure components implicated are AOS servers and IIS servers (TLS/cipher negotiation), TLS policy/configuration and Windows security updates as potential contributors; an internal JIRA (ICO261-183) from another region reported the same error, indicating a global or multi-region manifestation that requires coordinated configuration and testing across environments. Ownership and operational actions were recorded in portal notes (contact persons and consulting company involvement), and the ticket closure explicitly links the TLS/cipher change and server restarts to the remediation outcome. ## TI-722511 is a production-impacting, multi-environment incident resolved by TLS/cipher-suite changes and server restarts The ticket TI-722511 records reproducible errors accessing Financial Reports across UAT and PROD and documents closure text indicating that best-practice cipher suites were activated with IISCRYPTO and AOS servers were restarted as the remediation that resolved the issue. This linkage between the ticket, the tool (IISCRYPTO), the configuration change and the server restarts is explicitly documented in the closure notes and portal history [Data: Entities (0); Entities (18,20); Relationships (9,34,36)]. Because the ticket affects both UAT and PROD and includes symptom reports from EMEA finance users (report creators appearing as "unknown" and inability to open reports), this is a production-grade operational incident rather than a single-user problem; the ticket metadata and relationships show cross-environment impact and operator action items that are relevant to architecture and runbook updates [Data: Entities (0); Relationships (1,10)]. ## AOS and IIS servers are central operational dependencies for the reporting service AOS servers broker requests from user clients (browser/Report Designer) to backend reporting services and were explicitly restarted as part of the remediation, showing they are a required operational touchpoint when TLS/cipher changes are applied. The relationships document the dependency between AOS servers, users and TLS policy changes, and record that AOS restarts were necessary after cipher adjustments [Data: Entities (19,483,487); Relationships (796,798,36)]. IIS servers are also the target of IISCRYPTO changes and therefore form the TLS termination layer that directly affects client report access; the record linking IISCRYPTO to IIS servers and to the best-practice cipher-suite configuration confirms they are part of the change domain that produced the outage [Data: Entities (26,18,20); Relationships (35,34)]. ## TLS / cipher-suite configuration and Windows security updates are a plausible root-cause vector and must be part of diagnostics The dataset explicitly identifies TLS/cipher-suite configuration as a primary decision-tree item for multi-environment report failures and calls out that Windows security updates can alter available crypto libraries or TLS behavior, impacting negotiation and compatibility [Data: Entities (480,488); Relationships (803)]. Operationally this means any change to the TLS configuration (cipher suites or minimum TLS versions) or a cumulative OS/security patch may introduce incompatibilities between client/report-designer, IIS, AOS and backend services; the incident remediation (activating best-practice ciphers and restarting servers) and the diagnostic checklist in the records both point to these items as the first-line troubleshooting steps [Data: Entities (0,20,480,488); Relationships (9,803,36)]. ## The incident shows cross-regional recurrence — internal Jira ICO261-183 reports the same error An internal JIRA ticket (ICO261-183) opened by another internal team (Mexico) reported the same error, and the portal notes for TI-722511 reference that JIRA ticket, indicating the problem was observed outside a single operating region and should be treated as a global/tenant-level issue requiring coordinated remediation and communication [Data: Entities (17); Relationships (33); Entities (0); Relationships (0)]. This cross-reference implies the root cause is likely systemic (configuration or policy across environments) rather than an isolated host-level fault, elevating the need for a deployment-wide review of TLS/cipher policies, synchronized change windows, and shared runbooks to avoid reoccurrence [Data: Entities (17,0); Relationships (33,9)]. ## Consulting company acted as primary triage/coordination but operational ownership and escalation paths are fragmented Portal no-reply entries and the ticket relationships identify a consulting organization performing triage, posting portal updates and coordinating remediation activities for TI-722511, which indicates third-party involvement in operational execution [Data: Entities (24); Relationships (16)]. While external support can accelerate remediation, the records show multiple actors (customer contact, consulting company, internal teams, and referenced JIRA) are involved; this multiplicity increases coordination overhead and suggests the need to codify explicit escalation and handoff steps (who is authorized to change TLS settings, who validates post-change testing, and who owns cross-region communication) to reduce confusion during future incidents [Data: Entities (2,24,17); Relationships (1,16,33)]. ## The ticket closure documents a fix but there is limited explicit trace of pre- and post-change testing and rollback procedures Closure text states best-practice cipher suites were activated with IISCRYPTO and servers restarted, and this outcome is associated with resolution of the ticket, but the dataset does not include detailed pre-change compatibility testing results, a staged rollout record, or an explicit rollback plan linked to the change [Data: Entities (0,18,20); Relationships (9,34)]. For production TLS changes that can break client compatibility, the absence of documented staged testing or rollback guidance in the ticket and relationships represents an operational risk — future changes should include mandatory compatibility validation (Report Designer and browser tests across supported clients), versioned configuration snapshots, and an approved rollback procedure recorded in the change ticket before applying IISCRYPTO or similar tooling [Data: Entities (0,480,18); Relationships (9,34,35)]. ## Recommended immediate actions: formalize TLS change control, add AOS/IIS restart playbook, and update monitoring/alerts Given the documented remediation and dependencies, the highest-priority operational actions are: 1) formalize a TLS/cipher-suite change control process that enforces synchronized configuration across UAT and PROD and requires pre-change validation; 2) add a concise runbook for TLS changes that includes steps to use IISCRYPTO, how and when to restart IIS and AOS servers, and post-change verification for report access; and 3) tune monitoring/alerts to detect report designer failures, unknown report-owner symptoms, and TLS negotiation failures so teams can detect regressions quickly. These recommendations are directly derived from the incident facts that link TLS configuration, IISCRYPTO use, and server restarts to resolution [Data: Entities (0,18,20,19,26,480); Relationships (9,34,36,35,796)]. Implementing these controls will reduce time-to-detect and time-to-recover for similar incidents and make cross-team communication and escalation more predictable. ## Governance and long-term mitigation: standardize TLS baselines, document supported client profiles, and maintain a cross-region configuration registry The incident demonstrates the need for organizational governance around TLS baselines (approved cipher suites and minimum TLS versions) and explicit documentation of supported client versions for the Report Designer and browser clients. Records show the problem manifested across regions and environments and was tied to cipher-suite changes, reinforcing that a single source of truth for TLS configuration and supported client compatibility is required [Data: Entities (480,483); Relationships (803,796,36)]. A practical control is a configuration registry (or CMDB extension) that records TLS/cipher settings per environment and a pre-deployment verification checklist that gates any change; combine this with scheduled compatibility testing against a matrix of client versions to avoid breaking production users when adopting "best-practice" cryptographic settings that may remove older ciphers still required by legacy clients [Data: Entities (480,483); Relationships (36,796)]. ## Communication and escalation runbook: define stakeholder matrix and cross-reference internal tracking systems The records link the ticket to named customer contacts (Contact Person 7, Regina Jagsch), a consulting company, and an internal JIRA that reported the same error, indicating multiple stakeholders and tracking systems were in use during the incident [Data: Entities (2,11,24,17); Relationships (1,10,16,33)]. To streamline future incidents, create a short escalation runbook that maps roles (customer contact, platform-owner, consulting SPOC, security owner), primary communication channels, and which external/internal ticketing system must be updated (portal ticket and JIRA). Include templates for status updates and a requirement to escalate to platform engineering when TLS/cipher changes are proposed, ensuring a single coordinated change window and clear ownership for rollback and postmortem actions [Data: Entities (0,2,11,24,17); Relationships (1,10,16,33)].

REPORT 53: # Financial Reporting Service — Report Designer and AOS (001-SRV-AOS72/73) The community centers on REPORT DESIGNER (the client/editor) and the FINANCIAL REPORTING SERVICE runtime, which depend on AOS application servers (notably 001-SRV-AOS72 and 001-SRV-AOS73), the Data Mart/FRDB, SQL, and network/TLS/authentication plumbing. Users experience globally slow startup and rendering, intermittent "Report cannot be found" errors, and lost author metadata (authors shown as "unknown"). Remediation activity included FRDB recreation and Data Mart resets in the D365FO_INTE environment and a Microsoft support ticket was opened to investigate the global failures. Observations point to a mix of node-specific application-server degradation (72 vs 73), dependency failures affecting metadata lookup (service account/TLS/permissions), and potential network/latency factors that together produce the end-user symptoms. ## REPORT DESIGNER is tightly coupled to multiple backend dependencies (AOS, Data Mart/FRDB, SQL, and TLS/auth) and exhibits systemic symptoms. REPORT DESIGNER’s correct operation depends on successful TLS negotiation, service-account authentication/authorization, and reliable connectivity to AOS, the Data Mart/FRDB, and SQL. The symptom set reported (global slowness, "Report cannot be found", and missing author metadata) aligns with failures in one or more of those dependencies rather than purely a local client issue. This dependency surface makes the client sensitive to infrastructure, identity/auth, and database issues at multiple layers. The source material explicitly cites TLS compatibility, service account authentication, and reachability to AOS/Data Mart/SQL as required dependencies and diagnostic vectors for the observed problems. This implies remediation must treat authentication, certificate/TLS compatibility, and backend reachability as first-class troubleshooting targets rather than focusing solely on client-side tuning or UI fixes. [Data: Entities (34,477); Relationships (791,785)] ## Node-specific issue: 001-SRV-AOS72 behaves as a diagnostic fast-path while 001-SRV-AOS73 is sluggish — indicating a per-node degradation or configuration drift. When REPORT DESIGNER is launched directly on 001-SRV-AOS72 the client is performant, while normal (remote) access that routes through the service tier encounters global slowness and degraded behavior. This divergence between twin siblings suggests either a node-specific configuration, caching, or connectivity difference on 001-SRV-AOS73 (or the network path to it) rather than an application-wide bug. Relationships and entity descriptions identify 001-SRV-AOS72 as the node that returns acceptable performance and 001-SRV-AOS73 as the sluggish sibling implicated in degraded end-user experience; the pair are explicitly described as sibling application servers in the same tier. Investigation should therefore include node configuration comparison, local caches, certificate stores, service-account context, and network path/middleware (load balancer or firewall) differences between the two servers. [Data: Entities (35,36); Relationships (61,55)] ## Author metadata loss points to metadata store access or permission issues affecting existing reports. Reports are showing authors as "unknown", which is consistent with metadata being inaccessible or lost from the source store (or being present but unreadable due to permission/identity failures). The presence of a "PREVIOUS REPORT CREATORS" group and the explicit relationship noting these creators are now displayed as unknown indicate that author metadata either wasn’t migrated, became orphaned, or cannot be read by the reporting runtime due to permission or authentication issues. Restoring or validating metadata read paths requires validating the service account(s) used by the Financial Reporting Service and Report Designer have read access to metadata stores, and confirming that any database/schema changes (including FRDB recreation) preserved or remapped author identity attributes. Audit the metadata access patterns and permission mappings to determine whether the metadata still exists but is blocked, or was removed during prior maintenance. [Data: Entities (45,34); Relationships (58)] ## FRDB recreation and Data Mart resets in D365FO_INTE were used as remediation; this suggests data-store corruption or misalignment was suspected/observed. The FRDB instance in the D365FO_INTE environment was recreated by an engineer as part of remediation steps and the environment was used to manage FRDB/Data Mart artifacts. Recreating FRDB indicates the remediation team suspected the database schema or contents were out-of-sync or corrupted for reporting consumption, and used the integration/test environment to rebuild report data artifacts. While FRDB recreation can resolve schema or corruption issues, it also risks metadata loss or identity remapping if not coordinated with metadata and author attribution preservation. Any further remediation should include metadata validation, backups, and a reconciliation step to ensure recreated FRDB instances contain the expected author and metadata references or that mappings are restored. [Data: Entities (38,37); Relationships (60,62)] ## Network latency is an acknowledged contributing factor and must be ruled in/out as part of root-cause analysis. Network latency is explicitly included in the decision tree as a cause for extreme startup slowness of Report Designer. Given the multi-tier traffic pattern (client → Reporting runtime → AOS → Data Mart/SQL) even modest latency or intermittent path degradation can amplify startup delays and timeouts, worsening perceived performance. Investigations should collect latency and packet-path telemetry from representative clients to each app server (including 001-SRV-AOS72/73), and from app servers to Data Mart/SQL. Correlate latency spikes or retransmissions with user-reported slowdowns and with the times FRDB resets or configuration changes occurred to identify whether network factors coincidentally or causally contributed to the incidents. [Data: Entities (490,34); Relationships (791)] ## Microsoft support engagement indicates the issue crosses product boundaries and requires coordinated vendor troubleshooting alongside internal remediation. A Microsoft support ticket was opened specifically to investigate the global reporting/Report Designer failures and related symptoms. Escalating to Microsoft suggests the internal team reached a boundary where vendor insights (for the reporting product/runtime, TLS negotiation behaviors, or AOS interactions) were needed, and that the incident may involve platform-level behavior or documented product bugs. This necessitates a clear escalation and evidence package (repro steps, diagnostic logs from 72/73, TLS traces, service-account access failures, FRDB snapshots) to reduce time-to-resolution with the vendor. Maintain a central incident bundle and a named internal owner to coordinate evidence collection and vendor interactions to avoid duplicated effort and message fragmentation. [Data: Entities (34,477); Relationships (53)] ## Operational exposure: lack of clear ownership, cross-team handoffs, and potential for repeat incidents — prioritize dependency mapping, runbooks, and governance changes. The dataset shows multiple remediation actions (FRDB recreation, Data Mart reset), a Microsoft ticket, node-specific performance divergence, and metadata loss — a pattern that indicates gaps in runbook completeness, dependency visibility, and governance for change actions affecting reporting. Without a documented and practiced change plan and stakeholder matrix, FRDB/metadata changes risk recurrence or unintended data loss. Priority actions should include (1) an explicit dependency map linking REPORT DESIGNER → Financial Reporting Service → AOS nodes → Data Mart/FRDB → SQL → network/TLS/auth, (2) a stakeholder matrix naming owners for each layer (app, infra, DB, network, identity) and an escalation path (including vendor), (3) a rollback-capable change plan and runbook for FRDB/Data Mart work that preserves/validates metadata and author mappings, and (4) monitoring/alert tuning for node-level performance divergence (001-SRV-AOS72 vs 73). Implementing these will reduce mean time to detect and remediate recurrence. [Data: Entities (38,490); Relationships (62,53,55)]

These artifacts are the ones the incident analysis says to collect to prove TLS handshakes, node drift and FRDB access errors
context: SOURCE 25: ﻿ general info & description Management Reporting & Financial Reporting Infrastructure Standard Version 1.0 Date: 12.04.2024 Status: Approved This document describes the architecture, security configuration and operational standards for the Financial Reporting (Management Reporter / Financial Reports) infrastructure within the D365 Finance & Operations environment of Customer Company 11. The objective is to ensure: • Stable Report Designer startup • Access to existing reports • Consistent report metadata • High-performance report generation • TLS-compliant security configuration The reporting infrastructure depends on: • AOS servers • Financial Reporting Data Mart • IIS components • TLS / Cipher Suite configuration • Service accounts • SQL backend The system is configured consistently across environments (UAT, PROD). design Architecture overview User (Browser / Report Designer) → AOS Server (Application Object Server) → Financial Reporting Services → Data Mart (SQL) → IIS / TLS Layer Financial Reporting components 1. Financial Reporting Service Hosts Report Designer and Report Rendering Engine 2. Data Mart Provides aggregated financial data 3. AOS Server Handles authentication and communication 4. IIS Layer Responsible for TLS connections and Cipher Suites Security concept • TLS encryption according to company standard • Only defined Cipher Suites allowed • Best-practice configuration according to Microsoft recommendations • Uniform configuration across all environments Misconfigurations in TLS or Cipher Suites can result in: • Report Designer starting extremely slowly • Reports not loading • Authors appearing as “unknown” • Reports not being found Operational principles • Changes to Cipher Suites must be performed synchronously in all environments • After adjustments, a restart of the AOS servers is required • Data Mart reset is only relevant in case of data inconsistency • Global issues usually indicate infrastructure or security layer problems decision tree & use cases Initial question Does the issue occur only in one environment or globally (UAT + PROD)? A) Issue only in one environment 1. Check Data Mart reset 2. Check SQL connection 3. Check AOS service 4. Check IIS configuration B) Issue in multiple environments simultaneously → Infrastructure or security configuration likely Further checks: 1. Were Cipher Suites changed? 2. Were TLS policies adjusted? 3. Was a Windows security update installed? 4. Were IIS settings modified? C) Reports show “unknown” as author 1. Check service account connection 2. Check authentication handshake 3. Check TLS compatibility D) Report Designer extremely slow 1. Test direct access via AOS Server 2. Check network latency 3. Check TLS/Cipher negotiation E) Report not found 1. Check Data Mart consistency 2. Check report metadata 3. context: SOURCE 25: ﻿ general info & description Management Reporting & Financial Reporting Infrastructure Standard Version 1.0 Date: 12.04.2024 Status: Approved This document describes the architecture, security configuration and operational standards for the Financial Reporting (Management Reporter / Financial Reports) infrastructure within the D365 Finance & Operations environment of Customer Company 11. The objective is to ensure: • Stable Report Designer startup • Access to existing reports • Consistent report metadata • High-performance report generation • TLS-compliant security configuration The reporting infrastructure depends on: • AOS servers • Financial Reporting Data Mart • IIS components • TLS / Cipher Suite configuration • Service accounts • SQL backend The system is configured consistently across environments (UAT, PROD). design Architecture overview User (Browser / Report Designer) → AOS Server (Application Object Server) → Financial Reporting Services → Data Mart (SQL) → IIS / TLS Layer Financial Reporting components 1. Financial Reporting Service Hosts Report Designer and Report Rendering Engine 2. Data Mart Provides aggregated financial data 3. AOS Server Handles authentication and communication 4. IIS Layer Responsible for TLS connections and Cipher Suites Security concept • TLS encryption according to company standard • Only defined Cipher Suites allowed • Best-practice configuration according to Microsoft recommendations • Uniform configuration across all environments Misconfigurations in TLS or Cipher Suites can result in: • Report Designer starting extremely slowly • Reports not loading • Authors appearing as “unknown” • Reports not being found Operational principles • Changes to Cipher Suites must be performed synchronously in all environments • After adjustments, a restart of the AOS servers is required • Data Mart reset is only relevant in case of data inconsistency • Global issues usually indicate infrastructure or security layer problems decision tree & use cases Initial question Does the issue occur only in one environment or globally (UAT + PROD)? A) Issue only in one environment 1. Check Data Mart reset 2. Check SQL connection 3. Check AOS service 4. Check IIS configuration B) Issue in multiple environments simultaneously → Infrastructure or security configuration likely Further checks: 1. Were Cipher Suites changed? 2. Were TLS policies adjusted? 3. Was a Windows security update installed? 4. Were IIS settings modified? C) Reports show “unknown” as author 1. Check service account connection 2. Check authentication handshake 3. Check TLS compatibility D) Report Designer extremely slow 1. Test direct access via AOS Server 2. Check network latency 3. Check TLS/Cipher negotiation E) Report not found 1. Check Data Mart consistency 2. Check report metadata 3.
